title: Undoing Changes
subtitle: Your safety net

sections.content: Made a mistake? Git can **undo** changes to a file by restoring it to the last committed version. This is like a safety net — as long as you've committed recently, you can always get back to a known good state.
sections.analogy: Think of `git checkout --` as a Ctrl+Z for your entire file. If you accidentally deleted half your essay, you can restore it from the last photograph (commit) you took. The key is that you can only restore what you've previously saved.
keyPoints: `git checkout -- filename` restores a file to its last committed state
This discards all uncommitted changes to that file — they're gone permanently
This is why committing often is important: more commits = more restore points

sections.instruction: First, set up a git repository so we have a safety net.
sections.prompt: Type `git init` to initialize the repository.

onSuccess.message: Repository created. Let's save the current good version of index.html.
Committed! Git now has a safe copy of your working website.

sections.question: Why is committing frequently a good practice?
options: Git requires at least one commit per hour
More commits = more restore points if something goes wrong
It's required before you can use `git diff`
sections.explanation: Each commit is a snapshot you can return to. If you commit every hour versus once a day, you have many more restore points. Think of it as saving your game — the more often you save, the less progress you lose.

sections.instruction: Oh no! You accidentally overwrite the entire file with garbage.
sections.prompt: Type `echo "BROKEN" > index.html` to simulate a mistake.

onSuccess.message: The file is destroyed! All your HTML is gone, replaced with just 'BROKEN'.

sections.prompt: Type `cat index.html` to view the file contents.

onSuccess.message: The whole file is just 'BROKEN' now! In the old days, you'd have to retype everything. But git remembers the original...

sections.instruction: Use `git checkout` to restore the file to its last committed version. The `--` tells git you mean a file, not a branch.
sections.prompt: Type `git checkout -- index.html` to restore it.

onSuccess.message: The file is restored! Your HTML is back exactly as it was. `git checkout --` undoes all uncommitted changes to a file. This is your safety net.

completionMessage: `git checkout -- filename` restores a file to its last committed version. As long as you commit regularly, you can always undo mistakes. Git is your safety net.
